title: Let's Crack Gym
content: Five days a week, no excuses, no skipped sessions. This is my current split.       Before we get into it — the weight you use is completely on you. Don’t feel bad if you lift less than the guy next to you or your friend. Everyone starts somewhere. Everyone has different genetics, different history, different goals. The only person you’re competing with is who you were yesterday. Pick a weight that challenges you, not one that impresses someone else.      On sets and reps — the sets and reps I’ve listed are based on what works for me. Take them as a suggestion, not a rule. One thing I will say though — people do way too many sets. More sets does not mean more muscle. With a good weight and proper form, 3-4 sets per exercise is more than enough. Focus on quality over quantity. Ten sets of half-effort is worse than 3 sets of full effort.      On “Till Failure” — this means you push yourself as hard as you can until you physically cannot do another rep with good form. Don’t stop because it burns or because it’s uncomfortable — stop when you genuinely have nothing left. This is where real muscle growth happens. The last 2-3 reps you didn’t think you had in you are the ones that actually matter.      On muscle fiber stretch — the more slowly and fully you move through each rep, the more you stretch the muscle fiber. If you are not fully stretching the muscle at the bottom of the movement, you are leaving gains on the table. Half reps build half muscles. Go deep, go slow, feel the stretch — your gains will be on a completely different level.      On exercise rankings:    ⭐ Superior — This exercise has exceptional muscle contraction and feel. It hits the muscle the way it’s meant to be hit. If you’re short on time, always prioritize these.    🟣 Heavy — I recommend hitting this exercise as heavy as possible with progressive overload. These are your money movements — the ones where adding weight over time makes the biggest difference.    Progressive Overload explained — Progressive overload simply means doing a little more over time. That could be adding 2.5kg to the bar, doing one extra rep, or doing the same weight with better form. Your muscles grow when they’re forced to adapt to a new challenge. If you’re lifting the same weight with the same reps every single week, you’re not growing — you’re just maintaining. Track your lifts. Push a little more each week. That’s the entire secret to building muscle.     Monday — Chest / Front & Side Shoulders  The week starts with a push. Chest and shoulders set the tone.                 Exercise       Sets       Reps                               Flat Bench Press       3       🔴 Till Failure       🟣 Heavy                 Incline Dumbbell Press       4       🔴 Till Failure       ⭐ Superior                 Cable Chest Fly       3       12-15                         Seated Fly       3       12-15                         Shoulder / Military Press       4       🔴 Till Failure       ⭐ Superior 🟣 Heavy                 Lateral Raises       4       12-15       ⭐ Superior                 Front Raises (optional)       3       12                      Note on Front Raises: If you’re short on time, skip this one guilt-free. Your front shoulder gets trained on every single pressing exercise you do — bench press, incline press, military press. By the time you reach front raises, it’s already had a solid workout. Lateral raises and military press are the priority here.   Rest: Rest whenever you feel like you’ve had enough but watch the time. Don’t let rest become a scroll session. Focus on recovering, not disappearing.     On time: I don’t think any session should take more than 1 hour 15 minutes to wrap up. If it is, something is off — could be too many sets, too long on rest, or too much time on your phone between sets. In the beginning it’s completely fine, you might be exhausted after each set and need longer to recover. But as you progress, aim to keep it tight. In and out. Quality over duration.     Tuesday — Back / Rear Shoulders  Pull day. This is where you build the thickness and width that makes everything look bigger.                 Exercise       Sets       Reps                               Deadlift (optional)       4       6-8                         Lat Pulldown       4       🔴 Till Failure       ⭐ Superior 🟣 Heavy                 Chest Supported Row Machine       3       🔴 Till Failure       🟣 Heavy                 Chest Supported / T-Bar Row       3       🔴 Till Failure                         Barbell Shrugs       4       12-15                         Reverse Fly       4       15                         Hyperextension       3       12-15                      Note on Deadlift: Deadlift is optional and I mean that genuinely. You do not need to go heavier and heavier on deadlifts to build a great back. Believe me, I’ve been years into this. The ego lift culture around deadlifts has hurt more people than it has helped. A good lat pulldown, a solid row, and consistent progressive overload on supported movements will build your back just as well — with far less injury risk. If you enjoy deadlifting, go for it. If you don’t, skip it without guilt.    Note on Hyperextension: This is your lower back work. Don’t skip it. Most people ignore lower back and pay for it with pain later. Keep the reps controlled, don’t go heavy — feel the muscle, not the momentum.   Rest: Rest whenever you feel like you’ve had enough — but watch the time. Focus on recovering, not disappearing.     On time: Keep it under 1 hour 15 minutes. If you’re going longer, tighten your rest periods or cut a set.     Wednesday — Biceps / Triceps / Forearms  Arms day. Everyone’s favorite. Hit every angle.                 Exercise       Sets       Reps                               Hammer Curl       3       12                         Incline Dumbbell Curl       4       🔴 Till Failure       ⭐ Superior                 Cable Curl       3       12-15                         Tricep Pushdown       4       🔴 Till Failure                         Bodyweight Dips       4-5       🔴 Till Failure       ⭐ Superior                 Overhead Tricep Extension       3       12                         Wrist Curls       3       15                   Rest: Rest whenever you feel like you’ve had enough but watch the time. Focus on recovering, not disappearing.     On time: Keep it under 1 hour 15 minutes. If it’s going longer, you’re likely resting too long or doing too many sets.     Friday — Legs  Most people skip leg day. Most people also wonder why they look weird. Don’t skip leg day.                 Exercise       Sets       Reps                               Barbell Squat       5       🔴 Till Failure       🟣 Heavy                 Leg Extension       4       🔴 Till Failure                         Hamstring Curl       4       🔴 Till Failure       ⭐ Superior                 Hamstring Stretch       3       30 sec                         Calf Raises       4       20                      Note on Hamstring Curl: Don’t do this on the regular hamstring curl machine if you can avoid it. Instead, use the leg extension machine facing the other way — one leg at a time. The muscle contraction you get is so much better. It isolates the hamstring properly and you’ll actually feel it working. Try it once and you won’t go back.    Note on Calves: Calves are heavily influenced by genetics. Some people grow them just by walking, others train them for years and see minimal change. Don’t get discouraged. Keep doing calf raises consistently, go through the full range of motion, and let time do its thing. You can still build them, it just takes longer for some people.   Rest: Legs are brutal — rest whenever you feel like you’ve had enough. But watch the time. Focus on recovering, not disappearing.     On time: Legs can run long — but still try to keep it under 1 hour 15 minutes. If you’re going over, tighten your rest periods. In the beginning it’s fine, legs are exhausting and you’ll need more time to recover between sets.     Saturday — Abs  Core day. Short, focused, no fluff.                 Exercise       Sets       Reps                       Plank       3       60 sec                 Cable Crunch       3       15                 Hanging Leg Raise       3       15                 Russian Twist       3       20           Rest: Rest whenever you feel like you’ve had enough but watch the time. Focus on recovering, not disappearing.     On time: This one should be done in 40 minutes or less.     How to Up Your Game in Muscle Building & Recovery          Hit your daily protein goal. Generally 2.2g per kg of bodyweight or 1g per pound. Spread your protein sources throughout the day, don’t have it all in one meal.           Sleep is non-negotiable. You need at least 7 hours for good muscle recovery.           Drink enough water.           Micronutrients matter. People focus too much on macros and forget about micros.         Nutrition is the key  Personal Supplement Recommendations          Protein Powder — Use if you are not able to hit your protein goal through diet. Note for vegetarians: plant-based protein has an incomplete amino acid profile. Complete proteins like chicken and meat have all essential amino acids. If you can’t eat those, protein powder is a good option.           Omega-3 — Take it daily.           Multivitamin — Covers your micronutrient gaps.           Zinc & Magnesium — Take before bed. Helps with sleep quality and recovery.           Creatine Monohydrate — Go research this before you use it. It works. It is not a steroid. It will help you look more full. Use the monohydrate form — it’s the most researched one out there.        What I Wish I Knew When I Started Lifting          Don’t do cardio before weight training. Cardio depletes your energy before you even touch the weights. Save it for after.           You don’t actually need to warm up with light weights. In my personal opinion, unless you have a condition where you need to warm up your joints or muscles before training, you don’t need it. Just get into it.           Try to maintain or go above 10,000 steps a day. It keeps you active outside the gym, aids recovery, and burns calories without affecting your training.           Training abs is just as important as training any other body part. Don’t treat it as an afterthought. A strong core carries over to every single lift you do.           If your diet is not in order, don’t expect huge changes. Training is only one half of the equation. Diet is the other — and honestly, it might be the more important half. If you’re not eating right, you’re leaving most of your gains on the table.        My “Controversial” Takes on Gym Culture          The gym is not a socializing place. I have very little time for myself and I cannot afford to spend it standing around having long conversations. I go in, I train, I leave. I’ve seen it happen constantly — people just standing and talking for 20 minutes between sets, barely training at all. It’s fine to be friendly, but if you’re spending more time chatting than lifting, you’re not really training.           Half knowledge is dangerous. Gym culture is full of people confidently giving advice they half-heard from someone else or completely misunderstood. Bad advice spreads fast in gyms. Be careful who you take advice from. If someone can’t explain why something works, they probably don’t actually know.           Fitness influencers are lying to you — most of them. A huge number of young people watch fitness influencers who claim to have built their physique completely naturally, no steroids, no hormone injections. People see that and set goals based on a physique that isn’t achievable naturally. Then when they can’t reach it they get sad, frustrated, and quit. The physique you’re chasing might literally require pharmaceutical help to achieve. That’s not your failure — that’s a lie you were sold. Try to watch those influencers who are open about their steroid use. I personally watch and recommend More Plates More Dates, Daddy Noel, Renaissance Periodization, and Jeff Nippard.        Personal Bests 🏆                 Exercise       KG       LBS                       Bench Press       135 kg       297 lbs                 Squat       130 kg       286 lbs                 Deadlift       210 kg       462 lbs                 Military Press       90 kg       198 lbs                Enjoy The Process.

title: Debug Your Body: A Fitness Guide for Tech People
content: You can write great codebase, audit smart contracts, and takedown systems at 2AM but when was the last time you debugged your own body?  Most tech people treat their health like a legacy codebase: ignore it until it breaks. By then, the damage is already done. And no, coffee cant fix that damage.  This is your guide to fixing that.    1. The Sitting Problem  The average tech person sits for 10–14 hours a day. Your body was not built for this.  Sitting for long periods compresses your spine, tightens your hip flexors, weakens your glutes, and cuts off circulation to your legs. This will going to cause you problem in the future. The fix is simple: move every hour.  Personally i have decided to go for long walks, i try to go over my faily step count which is at the time of writing this is 12k steps/day. You should also try to see how many steps you do, cause on a busy work day even i used to have around 2k steps/day. Set a timer for 50 minutes. When it goes off, stand up, walk around for 5–10 minutes, stretch your hips and back, then get back to work. This one habit alone will make a massive difference.  A standing desk is a great investment too — you don’t have to stand all day, just alternate between sitting and standing every hour or two.    2. Your Eyes Are Burning Out  My phone screentime will be less than 1.30 hour but i am afraid to look at the screentime of my pc, it would be so much, and thats the problem cause our jobs require us to stare/write/build codebases/apps etc. Also few of us like to play games to chill so screentime is throught the roof.  Follow the 20-20-20 rule:  Other things that help:    Set your screen brightness to match your room lighting.   Use night mode or a blue light filter after sunset.   Blink more consciously, screen users blink 3x less than normal.   Keep your monitor at arm’s length and slightly below eye level.     3. Posture — Your Spine Is Not a Suggestion  Bad posture is an epidemic in tech. Most people work hunched forward, chin jutting out, shoulders rounded, lower back unsupported.  The ideal desk setup:    Monitor at eye level, not tilted up or down.   Chair adjusted so your feet are flat on the floor.   Back fully supported by your chair.     4. Working Out When You Have No Time  Check out my post this post i went into depth discussing about working out and nutrition: Let’s Crack Gym  The minimum effective dose: 4 days a week, 40–60 minutes, is enough to stay in great shape. The key is showing up consistently, not going all out and burning out.    5. Sleep — The Most Underrated Performance Tool  Most tech people are chronically sleep deprived and don’t even realize it. They’ve adapted to feeling tired and think it’s normal.  It’s not.  Ngl, feeling like sleeping is a luxury that i want to afford so badly.  Minimum: 7 hours. Optimal: 8–9 hours.  How to actually sleep better:    Stop screens 30–60 minutes before bed (blue light suppresses melatonin)   Keep your room cold, is optimal for sleep   Go to sleep and wake up at the same time every day, including weekends     6. Nutrition for a Sharp Mind  You can’t run demanding cognitive work on junk food. Your brain uses roughly 20% of your body’s energy — what you eat directly affects how well you think.  The basics:  Protein — Eat enough. Protein keeps you full, supports muscle, and stabilizes blood sugar. Aim for 0.8–1g per pound of bodyweight. Eggs, chicken, fish, Greek yogurt, legumes.  Don’t skip meals — Skipping meals crashes your blood sugar, which tanks focus and mood. Eat regularly.  Hydration — Even mild dehydration (1–2%) significantly impairs cognitive function. Drink water consistently throughout the day. If you’re relying on coffee for energy, you’re probably just dehydrated.  Limit ultra-processed food — These cause blood sugar spikes and crashes, leading to the afternoon energy slump most tech workers know too well.  Caffeine is a tool, not a crutch — One or two cups of coffee in the morning is fine. Chasing it throughout the day builds tolerance and disrupts sleep, leaving you more tired than before.    7. Mental Health & Burnout  Burnout is not a badge of honor. It is your system crashing.  Tech culture glorifies overwork — late nights, skipped weekends, “hustle”. But the human brain has limits. Push past them long enough and you stop being productive. You become slow, irritable, unmotivated, and prone to mistakes.    The Stack  If you do nothing else, do these five things:     Move every hour — stand up, walk, stretch   Exercise 3x a week — even 30 minutes of bodyweight work   Sleep 7–8 hours — non-negotiable   Drink water — before you reach for more coffee   Go outside — sunlight and fresh air are free   Your body is the machine that runs everything else. Maintain it like you maintain your systems — proactively, not reactively.  Take care of yourself.
